question: What is the time complexity of searching in a hash table?
variants: How fast is hash map search?

answer.spoken: Hash table search has O(1) average time complexity due to direct index calculation from the hash function. Worst case is O(n) if all keys collide.

sections.title: How It Works
sections.content: The hash function converts the key to an array index, allowing direct access.
points: Hash function maps key to index
Collisions handled by chaining or probing

question: Explain the difference between an array and a linked list.
variants: When to use array over linked list?

answer.spoken: Arrays provide O(1) random access but O(n) insertion. Linked lists have O(n) access but O(1) insertion when you have the node reference.
bullets: Arrays: contiguous memory, O(1) access, O(n) insert
Linked lists: scattered memory, O(n) access, O(1) insert at known position
Arrays better for random access, linked lists for frequent insertions

sections.title: Memory Layout
sections.content: Arrays use contiguous memory blocks while linked lists use nodes with pointers.
points: Linked lists can grow dynamically without reallocation

question: What is a binary search tree and what are its properties?

answer.spoken: A binary search tree is a tree where each node's left subtree contains only smaller values and right subtree contains only larger values. This enables O(log n) search in balanced trees.
bullets: Average O(log n) operations, worst O(n) if unbalanced

sections.title: BST Property
sections.content: For every node, all values in left subtree are smaller and all in right subtree are larger.

question: How does a heap data structure work?

answer.spoken: A heap is a complete binary tree where each parent is smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) than its children. It provides O(1) access to min/max and O(log n) insertion and deletion.
bullets: Complete binary tree with heap property
Min-heap: parent ≤ children, Max-heap: parent ≥ children

sections.title: Heap Operations
sections.content: Insert adds at end and bubbles up. Delete removes root and heapifies down.

question: What is a trie and when would you use it?
variants: When to use trie over hash map?

answer.spoken: A trie is a tree for storing strings where each node represents a character. It excels at prefix-based operations like autocomplete, with O(m) lookup where m is string length.
bullets: Root to leaf path forms a word
O(m) lookup, m is string length

sections.title: Use Cases
sections.content: Autocomplete, spell checking, IP routing tables, prefix matching.

question: Explain a graph and its representations.

answer.spoken: Graphs consist of vertices and edges. Two main representations: adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space with O(1) edge lookup, while adjacency list uses O(V+E) space and is better for sparse graphs.
bullets: Adjacency matrix: O(V²) space, O(1) edge check
Adjacency list: O(V+E) space, O(degree) edge check
Matrix for dense graphs, list for sparse graphs

sections.title: Representation Trade-offs
sections.content: Choose based on graph density and operation frequency.
points: Matrix: fast edge lookup, more memory
List: memory efficient, slower edge lookup

question: Explain the difference between BFS and DFS.
variants: When to use BFS over DFS?

answer.spoken: BFS explores level by level using a queue, finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs. DFS goes deep first using a stack or recursion, useful for topological sort and cycle detection.

sections.title: Use Cases
sections.content: BFS for shortest path, level-order. DFS for topological sort, cycle detection, path finding.
points: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs
DFS uses less memory for deep graphs

question: How does quicksort work and what is its time complexity?
variants: Why is quicksort O(n log n)?

answer.spoken: Quicksort picks a pivot, partitions elements around it, then recursively sorts sub-arrays. Average O(n log n), worst O(n²) with poor pivot selection. In-place with O(log n) stack space.
bullets: Divide and conquer with pivot partitioning
Average: O(n log n), Worst: O(n²)
In-place, not stable, O(log n) space

sections.title: Algorithm Steps
sections.content: 1. Choose pivot 2. Partition around pivot 3. Recursively sort partitions
points: Preferred over mergesort for arrays due to cache locality

question: Explain merge sort and when you would use it over quicksort.

answer.spoken: Merge sort divides the array in half recursively, then merges sorted halves. It's stable with guaranteed O(n log n) but uses O(n) extra space. Preferred for linked lists and when stability matters.
bullets: Stable sort, preserves equal element order

sections.title: When to Use
sections.content: Use mergesort for linked lists, external sorting, or when stability is required.
points: Better for linked lists (no random access needed)

question: What is dynamic programming and when should you use it?

answer.spoken: Dynamic programming solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing results to avoid recomputation. Use when problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems.

sections.title: DP Approach
sections.content: Identify base cases, define recurrence relation, decide memoization or tabulation.

question: How do you find the shortest path in a weighted graph?
variants: When to use Dijkstra vs Bellman-Ford?

answer.spoken: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a source using a priority queue, with O(E log V) complexity. Use Bellman-Ford for negative weights, Floyd-Warshall for all pairs.
bullets: Dijkstra: O(E log V), non-negative weights only
Floyd-Warshall: O(V³), all pairs shortest path

sections.title: Algorithm Selection
sections.content: Choose based on graph properties and requirements.

question: How do you detect a cycle in a linked list?

answer.spoken: Use Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm with two pointers - slow moves one step, fast moves two steps. If they meet, there's a cycle. O(n) time, O(1) space.
bullets: Two pointers: slow (1 step), fast (2 steps)

sections.title: Finding Cycle Start
sections.content: After detection, reset one pointer to head and move both at same speed - they meet at cycle start.
points: Mathematical proof based on cycle entry point

question: How do you reverse a linked list?

answer.spoken: Use three pointers: previous, current, and next. Iterate through the list, reversing each pointer. O(n) time, O(1) space for iterative approach.
bullets: Track previous, current, and next nodes
Reverse pointer direction at each step

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: At each node, save next, point current to previous, move pointers forward.

question: How do you find the kth largest element in an array?

answer.spoken: Use QuickSelect algorithm - partitioning like quicksort but only recursing on one side. Average O(n) time, O(1) space. Alternative: use a min-heap of size k for O(n log k).
bullets: Min-heap of size k: O(n log k)
Sorting: O(n log n) but simpler

sections.title: QuickSelect
sections.content: Partition array, if pivot position equals k, done. Otherwise recurse on relevant half.

question: How does binary search work?
variants: When can you use binary search?

answer.spoken: Binary search repeatedly divides a sorted array in half, comparing the middle element to target. O(log n) time complexity. Requires sorted data.

sections.title: Variations
sections.content: Find first/last occurrence, search in rotated array, find insertion point.
points: Can be applied to monotonic functions

question: What is the two-pointer technique?

answer.spoken: The two-pointer technique uses two indices to traverse data, often from opposite ends or at different speeds. Common for sorted arrays, linked lists, and string problems.
bullets: Reduces O(n²) to O(n) for many problems
Types: opposite ends, same direction, fast/slow
Used for pair sums, palindromes, cycle detection

sections.title: Common Patterns
sections.content: Opposite direction for two-sum sorted, same direction for removing duplicates, fast/slow for cycles.

question: How do you implement a sliding window?

answer.spoken: Sliding window maintains a subset of elements as a 'window' that slides across data. Fixed window uses two pointers moving together; variable window expands/contracts based on conditions.
bullets: Fixed size: both pointers move together
Variable size: expand right, contract left
O(n) for problems that would be O(n²) with brute force

sections.title: Pattern
sections.content: Expand window with right pointer, shrink with left when condition violated.

question: What is backtracking and when do you use it?

answer.spoken: Backtracking explores all potential solutions by building incrementally and abandoning paths that can't lead to valid solutions. Used for constraint satisfaction like N-Queens, sudoku, and permutations.
bullets: Used for permutations, combinations, constraint problems

sections.title: Pattern
sections.content: Make a choice, recurse, undo choice (backtrack) if it doesn't work.
points: More efficient than brute force due to pruning
Time complexity often exponential but practical

question: What is a stack and how is it used?

answer.spoken: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure with O(1) push and pop operations. Used for function calls, undo operations, expression evaluation, and DFS traversal.

sections.title: Applications
sections.content: Call stack, expression evaluation, bracket matching, DFS, undo functionality.
points: Convert recursion to iteration using explicit stack

question: What is a queue and what are its variants?

answer.spoken: A queue is a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure. Variants include priority queue for ordering by priority, deque for both-end operations, and circular queue for fixed-size buffers.
bullets: Variants: priority queue, deque, circular queue
Used for BFS, task scheduling, buffering

sections.title: Variants
sections.content: Priority queue orders by priority. Deque allows both-end operations. Circular queue reuses space.
points: Priority queue often implemented as heap

question: How do you find all pairs that sum to a target?

answer.spoken: Use a hash map to store seen numbers. For each number, check if target minus current exists in the map. O(n) time, O(n) space.
bullets: Hash map: O(n) time, O(n) space
Sorted array + two pointers: O(n log n)

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Store each number with its index. For each number, check if complement exists.
points: Hash map is most efficient for unsorted

question: How do you merge two sorted arrays?

answer.spoken: Use two pointers, one for each array. Compare elements and add the smaller one to the result. Continue until both arrays are exhausted. O(n + m) time complexity.
bullets: O(n + m) time, O(n + m) space

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Compare front elements, take smaller, advance that pointer, repeat until done.
points: Handle remaining elements after one array exhausted
Can merge in-place for one array with extra space

question: How do you find the longest common subsequence?

answer.spoken: Use dynamic programming with a 2D table. If characters match, add 1 to diagonal. Otherwise, take max of left or top cell. O(mn) time and space.

sections.title: Recurrence
sections.content: If chars match: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1. Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
points: Bottom-up fills table row by row

question: How do you find the longest palindromic substring?

answer.spoken: Expand around each center, considering both odd and even length palindromes. O(n²) time, O(1) space. Manacher's algorithm achieves O(n) but is more complex.
bullets: DP approach: O(n²) time and space

sections.title: Center Expansion
sections.content: For each position, expand outward while characters match. Track longest found.
points: Check both odd (single center) and even (double center)
Simple and efficient enough for most cases

question: What is a topological sort and when is it used?

answer.spoken: Topological sort orders vertices in a DAG so that for every edge u→v, u comes before v. Used for dependency resolution, build systems, and course scheduling. O(V+E) time.
bullets: Kahn's algorithm uses BFS with in-degree

sections.title: Kahn's Algorithm
sections.content: Track in-degrees. Process nodes with zero in-degree, reducing neighbors' in-degrees.
points: Detects cycles if not all nodes processed

question: What is a balanced binary tree?

answer.spoken: A balanced tree maintains height of O(log n), ensuring operations stay O(log n). AVL trees strictly balance with rotations. Red-Black trees are less strict but faster for insertions.
bullets: Height balanced: O(log n) operations guaranteed

sections.title: Balancing
sections.content: Both use rotations to rebalance after modifications.
points: Red-Black: used in most language standard libraries

question: How do you find the median of a data stream?

answer.spoken: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and min-heap for the upper half. Keep them balanced in size. Median is either the top of max-heap or average of both tops.
bullets: Max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half
O(log n) insert, O(1) find median

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Insert into appropriate heap, rebalance if needed, median is from heap tops.

question: How do you implement LRU Cache?

answer.spoken: Use a hash map for O(1) lookup and a doubly linked list for O(1) removal and insertion. Move accessed items to front, remove from back when capacity exceeded.
bullets: Most recent at head, least recent at tail

sections.title: Operations
sections.content: Get: move to front if exists. Put: add to front, evict from back if full.
points: Doubly linked list allows O(1) removal
Hash map stores key to node mapping

question: How do you serialize and deserialize a binary tree?

answer.spoken: Use preorder traversal with null markers. Serialize: visit nodes in preorder, mark nulls. Deserialize: rebuild using preorder sequence, consuming markers for null children.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Preorder: root, left, right with special marker for null nodes.
points: Queue or index for deserialize position

question: How do you find all subsets of a set?

answer.spoken: Use backtracking: at each element, choose to include or exclude it. Alternatively, use bit manipulation with 2^n iterations. Both produce 2^n subsets.
bullets: Bit manipulation: n-bit numbers represent subsets

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.content: Build subsets by including each element then backtracking.
points: Cascading: add each element to existing subsets

question: What is the coin change problem?

answer.spoken: Find minimum coins needed for a target amount. Use DP: for each amount from 1 to target, compute minimum coins using all denominations. O(amount * coins) time.
bullets: dp[i] = min coins for amount i
O(amount * coins) time and O(amount) space

sections.title: Recurrence
sections.content: dp[amount] = min(dp[amount - coin] + 1) for all coins

question: How do you find the maximum subarray sum?

answer.spoken: Use Kadane's algorithm: track current sum, reset to current element when sum becomes negative. Track global maximum throughout. O(n) time, O(1) space.

sections.title: Intuition
sections.content: Either extend previous subarray or start fresh at current element.

question: What is a disjoint set (Union-Find)?

answer.spoken: Disjoint Set Union tracks elements in disjoint sets with near O(1) union and find operations using path compression and union by rank optimizations.
bullets: Used for connected components, Kruskal's MST

sections.title: Optimizations
sections.content: Path compression: flatten tree on find. Union by rank: attach smaller tree under larger.

question: How do you find the number of islands in a grid?

answer.spoken: Use DFS or BFS from each unvisited land cell, marking all connected land as visited. Each DFS/BFS start is a new island. O(m*n) time.
bullets: O(m*n) time, O(m*n) space for visited

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Iterate grid, start DFS/BFS on unvisited land, increment island count.
points: Can modify grid in-place to mark visited

question: What is memoization?

answer.spoken: Memoization caches function results to avoid recomputation. It's the top-down approach to dynamic programming where you solve subproblems as needed and cache them.

sections.title: Memoization vs Tabulation
sections.content: Memoization: recursive, solves only needed subproblems. Tabulation: iterative, solves all subproblems.
points: Memoization easier to implement from recursion
Tabulation may be more efficient (no recursion overhead)

question: What is the time complexity of common operations on a BST?

answer.spoken: In a balanced BST, search, insert, and delete are all O(log n). In an unbalanced BST, worst case is O(n) when the tree degenerates to a linked list.
bullets: Balanced: O(log n) for search, insert, delete

sections.title: Balance Matters
sections.content: Self-balancing trees (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee O(log n).

question: How do you validate if a binary tree is a valid BST?

answer.spoken: Use recursion with min/max bounds. Each node must be greater than the max in left subtree and less than min in right subtree. Pass down bounds during traversal.
bullets: Track valid range for each node

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Pass min and max bounds to each recursive call, narrow bounds as you descend.
points: Alternative: in-order traversal should produce sorted output

question: How do you find the lowest common ancestor in a binary tree?

answer.spoken: Use recursion: if current node is p or q, return it. Recurse on both subtrees. If both return non-null, current is LCA. Otherwise, return the non-null result.
bullets: Return node if it's p or q
LCA is where both subtrees return non-null

sections.title: BST Optimization
sections.content: For BST, compare values to determine direction without exploring both subtrees.

question: How do you implement a min stack?

answer.spoken: Use a second stack to track minimum values. When pushing, also push to min stack if value is smaller or equal. When popping, also pop from min stack if values match.
bullets: Two stacks: main and min tracker

sections.title: Space Optimization
sections.content: Store pairs of (value, minAtThisPoint) or only push to min stack when value decreases.

question: How do you check if parentheses are balanced?

answer.spoken: Use a stack. Push opening brackets, pop and check match for closing brackets. Return true if stack is empty at end.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Opening brackets go on stack. Closing brackets must match top of stack.
points: Empty stack at end means balanced

question: How do you rotate an array by k positions?

answer.spoken: Use the reverse technique: reverse entire array, reverse first k elements, reverse remaining. O(n) time, O(1) space. Handle k > length by taking k mod length.
bullets: Reverse method: O(n) time, O(1) space
Extra array: O(n) time and space
k %= length to handle k > length

sections.title: Reverse Method
sections.content: Reverse all, reverse 0 to k-1, reverse k to end.
points: Works for both left and right rotation

question: How do you find the missing number in an array of 1 to n?

answer.spoken: Use math: sum of 1 to n is n*(n+1)/2. Subtract array sum to get missing number. O(n) time, O(1) space. Alternative: XOR all numbers with 1 to n.
bullets: XOR approach: a^a = 0, so XOR all

sections.title: XOR Method
sections.content: XOR all array elements with 1 to n. Duplicates cancel, leaving missing number.

question: How do you find the majority element?
variants: Element appearing more than n/2 times

answer.spoken: Use Boyer-Moore voting algorithm: maintain candidate and count. Increment count if same, decrement otherwise. When count is 0, switch candidate. O(n) time, O(1) space.
bullets: Hash map: O(n) time and space

sections.title: Boyer-Moore
sections.content: Majority element survives cancellation. Count its occurrences vs all others.
points: Verify result if majority not guaranteed

question: What is a segment tree?

answer.spoken: A segment tree allows O(log n) range queries and updates on an array. Each node stores aggregate info for a range. Built in O(n), queries and updates in O(log n).
bullets: O(n) build, O(log n) query and update
Used for range sum, min, max queries

sections.title: Structure
sections.content: Binary tree where leaves are array elements, internal nodes are aggregates of children.

question: How do you find the longest increasing subsequence?

answer.spoken: DP solution is O(n²). Optimal O(n log n) uses patience sorting: maintain tails array where tails[i] is smallest ending element of LIS of length i+1. Binary search for position.
bullets: Binary search: O(n log n) time

sections.title: Binary Search Approach
sections.content: Maintain array of smallest tail elements. Binary search to find position for each new element.
points: Length of tails is LIS length

question: How do you clone a graph?

answer.spoken: Use DFS or BFS with a hash map to track original-to-clone mapping. Clone nodes as visited, connect clones based on original edges.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Create clone when first visiting node, store in map. Connect neighbors using clones from map.

question: What is the 0/1 knapsack problem?

answer.spoken: Maximize value while staying within weight capacity. Use 2D DP where dp[i][w] is max value using first i items with capacity w. O(nW) time and space.
bullets: dp[i][w] = max value with items 1..i, capacity w

sections.title: Recurrence
sections.content: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i])
points: Space can be optimized to O(W)
Decision: take or skip current item

question: How do you implement a trie with insert, search, and startsWith?

answer.spoken: Each node has children map and end-of-word flag. Insert creates nodes along path. Search checks path exists and ends at word. StartsWith only checks path exists.
bullets: Insert: O(m), Search: O(m), StartsWith: O(m)

sections.title: Operations
sections.content: Insert: create missing nodes. Search: traverse, check isEnd. StartsWith: traverse only.
points: Support for delete with reference counting

question: How do you find the diameter of a binary tree?

answer.spoken: Diameter is the longest path between any two nodes. For each node, calculate left height + right height. Track maximum across all nodes. O(n) time.
bullets: Path may or may not go through root

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: DFS returns height, updates global diameter when path through current node is longer.

question: What is the edit distance problem?

answer.spoken: Find minimum operations (insert, delete, replace) to transform one string to another. Use DP with 2D table. O(mn) time and space.
bullets: dp[i][j] = min ops for first i chars to first j chars

sections.title: Recurrence
sections.content: If chars match: dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: 1 + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1])
points: Used in spell checkers, diff tools

question: How do you find the minimum window substring?

answer.spoken: Use sliding window with two pointers. Expand right to include all characters, contract left to minimize window while still valid. Track character frequencies with hash map.
bullets: Expand right until valid, shrink left to minimize

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Track required characters and matched count. Shrink window when all matched.
points: Two frequency maps: required and current
Update result when window is smaller

question: How do you find all anagrams of a pattern in a string?

answer.spoken: Use fixed-size sliding window equal to pattern length. Track character frequencies. When window frequency matches pattern frequency, record start index. O(n) time.
bullets: O(n) time, O(1) space for fixed alphabet

sections.title: Optimization
sections.content: Instead of comparing full maps, track count of matched characters.
points: Array for frequencies if limited alphabet

question: How do you find the next greater element?

answer.spoken: Use a monotonic decreasing stack. For each element, pop smaller elements from stack and record current as their next greater. Push current to stack. O(n) time.
bullets: Pop while stack top is smaller

sections.title: Monotonic Stack
sections.content: Stack maintains decreasing order. When larger element found, it's the answer for popped elements.
points: Can handle circular arrays by iterating twice

question: How do you trap rainwater?

answer.spoken: For each position, water trapped equals min of max heights on both sides minus current height. Two-pointer approach: track left and right max, process smaller side. O(n) time, O(1) space.
bullets: Water at i = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i]

sections.title: Two Pointer
sections.content: Process from lower side since water level is limited by lower max.
points: Alternative: precompute left and right max arrays O(n) space

question: How do you merge overlapping intervals?

answer.spoken: Sort intervals by start time. Iterate through, merging with previous if overlap detected (current start <= previous end). Update end to max of both ends. O(n log n) for sorting.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: After sorting, check each interval against the last merged one.
points: Update end to max of overlapping intervals
Add to result when no overlap

question: What is Kruskal's algorithm for minimum spanning tree?

answer.spoken: Sort all edges by weight. Add edges to MST using Union-Find, skipping edges that create cycles. Stop when V-1 edges added. O(E log E) time complexity.

sections.title: Greedy Choice
sections.content: Always pick minimum weight edge that doesn't create cycle.

question: How do you find the longest substring without repeating characters?

answer.spoken: Use sliding window with a set or map to track characters. When duplicate found, shrink window from left until unique again. Track maximum window size. O(n) time.

sections.title: Optimization
sections.content: Use map storing last index of each character to jump left pointer directly.
points: Set approach: remove one at a time
Map approach: jump to last index + 1

question: How do you perform level-order traversal of a binary tree?

answer.spoken: Use a queue for BFS. Process nodes level by level, adding children to queue. Track level boundaries by processing queue size at start of each level.

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Enqueue root. For each level, dequeue all current nodes, process, enqueue children.
points: Alternative: track depth with each node

question: How do you detect a cycle in a directed graph?

answer.spoken: Use DFS with three states: unvisited, in current path, visited. If you reach a node in current path, there's a cycle. Alternative: Kahn's algorithm fails to process all nodes if cycle exists.
bullets: Cycle if gray node reached again
Kahn's: cycle if not all nodes processed

sections.title: DFS Coloring
sections.content: White: unvisited. Gray: in current DFS path. Black: fully processed.
points: Back edge to gray node = cycle
Convert gray to black after processing

question: How do you find the product of array except self?

answer.spoken: Compute prefix products from left, then suffix products from right. For each position, multiply its prefix and suffix. O(n) time, O(1) extra space if using output array.

sections.title: Without Division
sections.content: First pass stores product of all elements to the left. Second pass multiplies by product of all to the right.
points: Can use output array for O(1) extra space

question: How do you find the first and last position of an element in a sorted array?

answer.spoken: Use binary search twice: once to find leftmost occurrence (search left even when found), once for rightmost (search right when found). O(log n) time.

sections.title: Binary Search Variations
sections.content: Modify standard binary search to not stop when element found.

question: How do you search in a rotated sorted array?

answer.spoken: Modified binary search: check which half is sorted, then determine if target is in that half. If in sorted half, search there; otherwise search the other half. O(log n) time.
bullets: Check if target in sorted half

sections.title: Approach
sections.content: Compare mid with ends to identify sorted half, then check if target falls in that range.

question: How do you find the maximum profit from stock prices?
variants: Best time to buy and sell stock

answer.spoken: Track minimum price seen so far. At each day, calculate profit if selling today and update maximum. Single pass O(n) solution.

sections.title: Single Transaction
sections.content: Buy low, sell high - find the maximum difference where sell comes after buy.
points: Multiple transactions: sum all positive differences
At most k transactions: DP approach

question: What is a bloom filter?

answer.spoken: A bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure that tests set membership. It may have false positives but never false negatives. Uses multiple hash functions and a bit array.
bullets: False positives possible, no false negatives

sections.title: How It Works
sections.content: Insert: hash item k times, set those bits. Query: check all k bits are set.
points: Cannot delete elements (use counting bloom filter)
Used in databases, caches, network routers

question: What is the difference between greedy and dynamic programming?
variants: When to use greedy over DP?

answer.spoken: Greedy makes locally optimal choices hoping for global optimum - faster but doesn't always work. DP considers all subproblems for guaranteed optimal solution. Use greedy when greedy choice property holds.
bullets: Greedy faster but not always correct

sections.title: When to Use Each
sections.content: Greedy: activity selection, Huffman coding. DP: knapsack, shortest path (general).
points: Prove greedy choice property for greedy
